summary: The author achieved 9th place by combining three parallel pipelines: generative test-time training, DeBERTa-based classification, and a BGE retrieval model trained with triplet loss. Key technical choices included switching to Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507, optimizing training with Unsloth, removing noisy subreddit metadata, and applying mean pooling with discriminative learning rates to the classification head. The final submission used a weighted ensemble prioritizing the generative component (70% weight), with rank normalization and self-ensembling across different training configurations and random seeds. The approach highlights the critical need for data volume control and noise reduction during generative test-time training.
extensive_summary:
## Overview
The solution integrates three distinct components to tackle the LLM competition: generative models, classification models, and Faiss-based retrieval. Rather than relying on a single architecture, the author systematically optimized each pipeline and combined them through a structured ensemble strategy.

## Generative Models
The generative component was built on a test-time training approach, initially using Llama 3.2 3B. The author replaced it with Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 and restricted training to only 5% of the test data to prevent overfitting. Training was accelerated using Unsloth, cutting runtime significantly while improving the leaderboard score. A crucial insight was removing subreddit information, which acted as pure noise during test-time training and unnecessarily extended runtime. To stabilize training and increase diversity, the author deduplicated all examples and randomly matched positive/negative samples for each input. Ensembling Qwen3-4B, Llama 3.2 3B, and Phi-3.5-Mini-Instruct yielded strong results, with additional parallel training of Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct in the final submission.

## Classification Models
The classification pipeline leveraged DeBERTa-v3-base, E5-base, and MPNet-base. Optimizations focused on faster convergence and better representation learning: applying a 2x learning rate specifically to the classification head, switching from CLS token pooling to mean pooling, and implementing discriminative learning rates (LLDR) across transformer layers. These adjustments improved both public and private leaderboard scores with minimal runtime overhead.

## Retrieval Component
The third component utilized a BGE model trained with triplet loss, building on prior public work. At inference, the author ensembled single centroid and UMAP clustering methods to enhance retrieval quality. Despite its lower individual weight, this component contributed to the overall robustness of the final submission.

## Final Submissions & Ensembling
Two submission strategies were evaluated. Both followed a consistent pipeline: internal model ensembling per component, rank normalization within each part, and weighted averaging across parts (0.7 × Generative + 0.25 × Classification + 0.05 × Retrieval). Submission 2 introduced self-ensembling by training each generative model on two different data configurations, effectively doubling the generative ensemble size. Both submissions achieved ~0.933 public LB and ~0.927 private LB.

## Lessons Learned
The author explicitly documented several failed attempts, including two-stage training, semantic matching for positive/negative pairs, rule-specific prompts, label smoothing, logit-based ensembling, layer freezing, SENet fusion of pooling methods, and rule-based ensemble weighting. These failures underscored the importance of data stability, architecture-specific tuning, and performance-driven ensemble weighting.
competition_flow: Raw data was deduplicated and split into randomly matched positive/negative samples, fed into three parallel pipelines (generative test-time training, DeBERTa classification, and BGE retrieval), and the outputs were combined via rank normalization and weighted averaging to produce the final submission.
data_processing: Randomly matched positive/negative samples for each example to increase diversity
Removed subreddit information to eliminate training noise
Restricted generative test-time training to 5% of the test data
Applied mean pooling instead of CLS token pooling for classification representations
ensembling: Models were internally ensembled per component, followed by rank normalization within each part, then combined using weighted averaging (0.7 × Part 1 + 0.25 × Part 2 + 0.05 × Part 3), with generative models self-ensembled across different training data configurations and random seeds.
insights: Generative test-time training requires strict data volume control to avoid overfitting to repeated or noisy examples.
Mean pooling consistently outperformed CLS token pooling for representation learning in classification heads.
Applying a 2x learning rate to the classification head significantly accelerated convergence without destabilizing training.
Discriminative learning rates across transformer layers improved representation quality across all classification models.
Removing noisy metadata like subreddit information can simultaneously reduce runtime and improve leaderboard scores.
critical_findings: Generative models were extremely sensitive to data volume during test-time training, making deduplication and random positive/negative matching essential for stability.
Ensembling newly trained models with the original public notebook models yielded higher scores than the original approach alone, highlighting the value of iterative test-time refinement.
Rule-based ensemble weighting failed completely, demonstrating that performance-driven weight tuning is necessary when combining heterogeneous model families.
what_did_not_work: SENet fusion of CLS, mean pooling and max pooling

other_solution_refs.description: Triplet loss and Faiss retrieval pipeline
Alternative triplet loss and Faiss implementation
